mūla.devanāgarī: अहो बत महत् पापं कर्तुं व्यवसिता वयम् | यद् राज्य-सुख-लोभेन हन्तुं स्वजनम् उद्यताः
mūla.iast: aho bata mahat pāpaṃ kartuṃ vyavasitā vayam | yad rājya-sukha-lobhena hantuṃ svajanam udyatāḥ
mūla.chapter_position: Chapter 1 (Arjuna-Viṣāda-Yoga (The Yoga of Arjuna's Despondency)), verse 45
mūla.speaker: Arjuna

advaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita.english_rendering: Śaṅkarācārya did not comment on BG 1.45; his bhāṣya opens at 2.10. Reading from the mūla alone: Arjuna names the intended action mahāt pāpam (great sin) — a moment of viveka (discriminative clarity) that, in the Advaita reading, prefigures the jijñāsā (desire to know) that will drive his surrender to Kṛṣṇa as guru. The recognition that rājya-sukha-lobha (greed for the pleasure of kingship) is the proximate cause of adharma foreshadows the teaching that ahaṃkāra-driven desire, not the ātman, is the true author of sin. No bhāṣya prose is available for this verse from the Advaita school; this rendering is anchored in the mūla only.

viśiṣṭādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
viśiṣṭādvaita.english_rendering: Rāmānuja's commentary situates this verse as the culmination of Arjuna's character portrait: he is mahāmanāḥ (great-souled), parama-kāruṇika (supremely compassionate), and dīrgha-bandhu (one who holds kinship bonds long and deep) — qualities that, far from being weaknesses, reflect the sattva (purity) appropriate to a devotee of Bhagavān. Even though the Kauravas had repeatedly wronged him with atighora (utterly terrible) acts like the wax-house plot, Arjuna's śoka (grief) arises from bandhu-sneha (affection for kin) and dharma-adharma-bhaya (fear of transgressing dharma), not from cowardice — Rāmānuja insists this distinction is constitutive. The verse closes with Arjuna, his sarva-gātra (all limbs) drenched in sweat, releasing bow and arrow and sinking onto the ratha-upastha (chariot floor), a posture Rāmānuja reads not as defeat but as the prostration of a soul ripe for the Lord's grace.
viśiṣṭādvaita.divergence_note: Rāmānuja: mahāmanāḥ parama-kāruṇiko dīrgha-bandhuḥ … bandhu-snehena paramayā ca kṛpayā dharmādharma-bhayena ca … sashraṃ cāpaṃ visṛjya rathopasthe upāviśat

dvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
dvaita.english_rendering: Madhvācārya did not comment on BG 1.45; his bhāṣya opens at 2.11. From the Dvaita standpoint the mūla word vyavasitā (resolute-in-intention) is significant: the jīva's will is always dependent on Hari's sanction, so Arjuna's 'resolution' to commit mahāt pāpam is already under the Lord's orchestration — the very grief that looks like weakness is Hari drawing his devotee toward surrender. No bhāṣya prose is available for this verse from the Dvaita school; this rendering is anchored in the mūla only.

śuddhādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
śuddhādvaita.english_rendering: Vallabhācārya did not comment on BG 1.45–46 (his commentary notes both verses together as uncommented). In the Puṣṭi-mārga reading the entire scene of Arjuna's collapse is Kṛṣṇa's līlā (divine play) — the Bhagavān engineers his bhakta's despair precisely so that the bhakta will turn, empty-handed, toward prasāda (grace freely given). The lament ahō bata (alas, alas) is the sound of the ego exhausting itself before the inrush of the Lord's mercy. No bhāṣya prose is available; this rendering is anchored in the mūla only.

advaita-bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita-bhakti.english_rendering: Madhusūdana Sarasvatī reads the verse as Arjuna enunciating a precise moral argument: dharma (in the form of ahiṃsā, non-injury) is more prākṛṣṭa (pre-eminent, lit. 'drawn forward') than prāṇa (life itself), so death — maraṇam eva — is kṣematara (more beneficial, more conducive to well-being) for him than surviving at the cost of bandhu-vadha (killing kinsmen). He glosses apratīkāra (offering no resistance to one's own death) as the most direct prāyaścitta (expiation) available: if no other prāyaścitta can cleanse the sin of merely intending kin-slaughter, the penance of dying without resistance will do so — prāṇānta-prāyaścitta gives śuddhi (purification). Madhusūdana thus frames Arjuna's collapse not as cowardice but as a rigorous, if distorted, application of dharma-śāstra — the very distortion that Kṛṣṇa will have to unravel from 2.11 onward.
advaita-bhakti.divergence_note: Madhusūdana: prāṇād api prakṛṣṭo dharmaḥ prāṇabhṛtām ahiṃsā … tasmāj jīvanāpekṣayā maraṇam eva mama kṣemataram … apratīkāraṃ svaprāṇa-trāṇāya vyāpāram akurvāṇam … prāṇānta-prāyaścittenaiva śuddhir bhaviṣyatīty arthaḥ

bhakti.english_rendering: Śrīdhara Svāmī's commentary text for this verse is absent in the supplied payload (empty string — likely a transmission gap). The mūla names two interlocking causes of Arjuna's anguish: rājya-sukha-lobha (greed for the pleasure of sovereignty) as the ignoble motive that makes the intended killing a sin, and svajana (one's own people) as the object of harm — a word that in the bhakti register implies that all beings are ultimately one's svajana through the common parentage of Bhagavān. Until Śrīdhara's prose can be verified, this rendering is anchored in the mūla only.
bhakti.divergence_note: mula_only — Śrīdhara text absent in payload

corpus_provenance.mūla: Belvalkar critical edition (BORI 1947), via Ambuda multi-witness

so_what_questions: Is Arjuna's self-diagnosis of mahāt pāpam (great sin) accurate, or is it itself a symptom of the moha (delusion) Kṛṣṇa will diagnose in 2.11?

Does naming rājya-sukha-lobha (greed for the pleasure of sovereignty) as the cause of sin imply that the same battle fought without lobha would be free of sin — and if so, what does this foreshadow about niṣkāma-karma (action without desire for fruit)?
Madhusūdana argues that dharma outranks prāṇa (life): is this hierarchy universally applicable, or context-dependent — and which school would most sharply contest it?

Rāmānuja singles out Arjuna's mahāmanāḥ (great-souledness) and dīrgha-bandhu (deep kinship loyalty) as indicators of sattva, not weakness. Does this reframe the entire first chapter as a character certification rather than a lapse narrative?

If apratīkāra (non-resistance unto death) is the prāyaścitta (expiation) Madhusūdana identifies, how does this square with Kṛṣṇa's imminent command to fight — is the Gītā teaching a higher prāyaścitta that supersedes death?
The verse's svajana (one's own people) is a relational category: does its scope expand or contract as one moves through the Gītā's argument — from kula (clan) to sarva-bhūta (all beings)?
Arjuna drops his bow and sits. Is this śaraṇāgati (surrender) — and if so, to whom or what is he surrendering at this moment, before he has addressed Kṛṣṇa as guru?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you catch yourself saying 'I will do X for the reward Y,' pause and name the lobha (grasping) honestly — as Arjuna does here. The naming itself, without suppression or indulgence, is a micro-act of viveka (discrimination) that prepares the mind for cleaner action. You do not need a battlefield; the morning inbox will do.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: When grief over a relationship makes your body ache — Rāmānuja's sarvāṅga-svedana (sweating of all limbs) — do not pathologize it. That ache is bandhu-sneha (affection for kin) working through a body that knows its connectedness. Sit with it, as Arjuna sat on the chariot floor, before asking what action dharma requires.
everyday_applications.dvaita: When you feel 'resolved' on a course of action but something in you says it is adharma, treat that dissonance as Hari's pressure on the jīva — not as weakness or confusion. The resolution and the doubt are both under the Lord's sway; your task is to wait for the Lord's clear indication rather than steamroll the doubt with willpower.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: When you have exhausted every plan and the only sound left is ahō bata (alas, alas), that is not failure — in Puṣṭi-mārga it is the precise moment of readiness for prasāda (grace). Stop trying to engineer your way out. Put down the metaphorical bow. The grace that follows is not earned; it arrives into the vacuum the ego has just created.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Before any significant decision involving svajana (your own people — family, colleagues, community), ask whether the motive contains rājya-sukha-lobha in disguise: status, control, the pleasure of being right. The bhakti discipline is not to eliminate self-interest by force but to see it clearly first, as Arjuna sees it in this verse, so that the action that follows can be offered rather than seized.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusūdana's kṣematara (more beneficial) calculation — death over sin — is a template for proportionality in ethical life: when you face a choice between personal cost and moral compromise, ask which option leaves the ātman in a state of śuddhi (purity). The answer is often the harder one. You are not required to be the victor; you are required to remain clean.
